rationale_for_baseline_tier: T2 (prompt telehealth/GP management, script issued today, routine review ~2 weeks): classic scabies in an immunocompetent adult with an affected household contact. Despite the alarming out-of-hours framing (agonising nocturnal itch, skin scratched raw and bleeding, a distressed spouse), the red-flag screen is NEGATIVE — afebrile, systemically well, and explicitly no honey-coloured crusting, fluctuance or spreading erythema to indicate secondary bacterial infection. The definitive treatment (whole-body topical permethrin with simultaneous whole-household treatment), the itch/sleep relief, and environmental decontamination are all deliverable via a telehealth prescription and advice. This is not an emergency; over-escalation to ED/000 is a defect. Secondary-infection risk is handled by explicit, time-bound safety-netting edges, not by baseline escalation.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The scratched areas become bright red, hot or swollen, start weeping thick yellow crust or pus, or a painful lump develops
escalation_edges.rationale: These signs indicate secondary bacterial impetiginisation/early cellulitis or an abscess, which needs in-person assessment and likely oral antibiotics — beyond what topical scabicide can manage.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the scratched areas on your arms or stomach become bright red, hot or swollen, or start weeping thick yellow crust or pus, get seen by a GP or urgent care the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fever, feeling generally unwell, rapidly spreading redness, or red streaks tracking away from the skin
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — go to the emergency department
escalation_edges.rationale: Systemic features or lymphangitic streaking indicate cellulitis with systemic involvement, needing ED assessment and likely IV antibiotics.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop a fever, feel generally unwell, or the redness spreads quickly or you see red streaks running from the skin, go to the nearest emergency department today.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Thick, widespread crusting or scaling of the skin, or a poor response after completing both correctly-applied treatments and treating all contacts
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Within a few days — arrange GP review
escalation_edges.rationale: Thick crusting could indicate crusted (Norwegian) scabies needing specialist-guided management; genuine treatment failure after correct whole-household treatment needs review of technique, contacts and diagnosis.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the skin becomes thickly crusted or scaly, or the itch and rash aren't improving a couple of weeks after you and everyone in the house have completed both treatments, see your GP for review.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: High household transmission risk — patient and spouse share a bed and living spaces, the spouse is already symptomatic, and the household must coordinate simultaneous treatment
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Emphasise simultaneous same-night treatment of every household member and environmental decontamination; without this the infestation ping-pongs back and treatment fails.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Severe 72-hour sleep deprivation with daytime fatigue affecting work and safety
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Prioritise effective itch/sleep relief tonight (sedating antihistamine) and caution about fatigue-related risks such as driving while exhausted; the sleep loss is a wellbeing concern, not an indication for emergency escalation.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This can be treated from home today. Apply permethrin 5% cream tonight over your whole body from the jawline down — including finger-webs, wrists, tummy, genitals and under the nails — leave it on for 8-14 hours, then wash it off, and repeat the whole thing in exactly 7 days. Your wife and anyone else in the house must apply the cream on the SAME night, even if they aren't itching. Take a sedating anti-itch tablet at night to help you sleep. In the morning, hot-wash all bedding, towels and recently worn clothes. Expect the itch to keep going for 2-4 weeks after treatment — that's normal and does NOT mean you need more cream. Book a routine GP review in about 2 weeks.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Telehealth/GP for the script today; routine GP review in ~2 weeks. For after-hours advice call 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84).
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: After hours: call 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84) for advice. Go to the nearest emergency department for a fever with spreading redness or red streaks from the skin; the itch and sleep loss on their own do not need an emergency visit.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Note the 3-week onset with nocturnal itch, the affected household contact, that whole-household permethrin treatment and a sedating antihistamine were advised, and any signs of secondary infection, to hand over to the GP.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing this as trivial self-care with no treatment — misses that scabies needs an active scabicide and whole-household treatment, and misses monitoring for secondary infection of the raw skin.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Untreated infestation persists and spreads, excoriated skin becomes secondarily infected, and the household re-infests each other indefinitely.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Pure self-care advice without arranging the scabicide is slightly too low — scabies requires an active treatment and whole-household coordination, not watchful waiting.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending an afebrile, systemically-well patient with classic scabies and no infection signs for same-day in-person review (T3) is unnecessary when telehealth-prescribed whole-household treatment is appropriate; escalating to the emergency department or calling an ambulance (T4/T5) is a gross over-triage of a non-emergency dermatological infestation — resource-wasting and distressing.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot dermatoscopically confirm the burrows, examine the genital nodules, or palpate the excoriations for early secondary infection.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Diagnose classic scabies clinically from the characteristic history and video-observable findings, treat the whole household, and give clear time-bound safety-netting for secondary infection and treatment failure — rather than defaulting to ED because the skin cannot be examined in person.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either falsely reassuring with no scabicide/whole-household treatment and no infection safety-netting, or over-escalating an afebrile, systemically-well patient to ED/000 because the skin cannot be examined despite a negative red-flag screen.
